turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: These - go verbs, these verbs that take - go instead of - o for the I version in the present, also behave a little bit strangely in the future tenses. In the will and would tenses. So, we can look at that now, and whilst looking at it, revise the endings that we learned for will and would. So we said that our will endings, what we add on to the to form of the verb to get will, are the same as those very irregular versions of haber in the present. So we can make that connection to help us internalize them. Can you talk me through those will endings?

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: So it’s - e for I and - emos for us.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: For we, very good.

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: For we. - a for he, she, it, and you formal, - an for they.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: They or you plural, and the last one, you informal?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So we have the endings. And our endings for would, which again we are adding on to the to form of the verb, we need to remember one key sound for would. If we remember how to say I, that’s a nice hook to remember the key sound. So what is the key sound? would like

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: - ía. And then on to - ia we can add on, of course, our - mos or - n or - s if we need to. Good. So - go verbs, these verbs that have - go and then - ga for the mood tense, when they are building the futures, they have a contraction. But, they all make the same contraction. For example, I will have is tendré. Tendré.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Tendré tendré. So that last e of tener turns into a d, and we get tendré. This is a contraction because it means we end up with two syllables instead of three. It would be “teneré”, but it’s tendré. Which just makes everything a little bit more efficient. But all of these verbs, these - go verbs, are behaving in a similar way. So we have to come:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Venir. And I will come:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Vendré. Very good. I would come - the same contraction for both futures:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Vendría. Good. I would come. We would come:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Vendríamos. have time:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Vendríamos más tarde, pero no tenemos tiempo

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Vendríamos más tarde, pero no tenemos tiempo. What was to put?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: And I put:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Pongo. So this - go alerts us to the fact that this verb will behave in this irregular way as well. So if you want to say I will put it here, how will it be? I will put it here:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Lo pondré aquí. I would put it here, I would put it here:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. We would put it here:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Lo pondríamos aquí. And we will put it here:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Lo pondremos aquí. What was to leave or to go out?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Salir. I leave or I go out:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Salgo. So again this - go can set off our little alarm bell that this verb will probably be irregular in the futures. So how would you say I would leave or I would go out?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Saldría. Good. You would leave or you would go out (informally):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Saldrías. Will you leave? Will you go out tonight? Will you go out tonight?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: That’s would you, now we want will you:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Saldrás esta noche? But of course we have esta noche which is future context so we could just use the present. We could just say You go out tonight? We don’t need the future, so how would that be?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Sales esta noche? Good. So for most - go verbs, we take off the - go and we put - dré for I will doesn’t do it. and - dría for I would, in most of them. Traer, to bring, Traeré, I will bring. Atraeré, I will attract. Distraeré, I will distract. But most of these that have - go will go to - dría or - dré. Hacer and decir, to do or to make or to say or to tell, hacer and decir are particularly irregular. And we’ve already seen this. We’ve seen how after haber they become hecho and dicho. It’s very irregular. We saw how the informal command from hacer is haz, h-a-z. Again irregular, and here. So it’s not following this rule. It’s even more irregular. We have as well. I will do is haré haré. Haré. I will do or I will make.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: H-a-r-e. Haré. And all we need to do is identify one of these irregularities and then we understand that all of will and all of would will use this same base, the same base that haré has used. So we will do or we will make is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And we would do or we would make:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Haríamos.: S: Lo haríamos, pero estamos-

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Occupied.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Lo haríamos, pero estamos ocupados ahora.. So haríamos is we would do or we would make, and what is we will do or we will make?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Haremos. And they will do, they will make:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Harán. I would say or I would tell is diría. So we get from decir, diría. I would say, he would say, she would say, you (formal) would say:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So like I said, we just need one of these versions to understand how the rest will be. So if diría is I would say or I would tell, how is I will say or I will tell?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Diré. I will tell you later (speaking informally):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Te diré más tarde. And of course, having future context with más tarde, we could just say:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Te digo más tarde. I tell you later. What was to be able to?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Poder. How do you say I would be able to or I could?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Podría. Good. The / e / falls out. So this is a different irregularity. This is not a - go verb. Podría. Of course it was “podería”, but many years of “podería, podería, podería, podería”, the / e / fell away. Podría. How is to put?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Poner. And I would put:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Pondría. Good. So there’s a subtle difference in sound between I would be able to, podría, and I would put, pondría. We would be able to:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: We would be able to. That’s we would put.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Podríamos. And we would put:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Pondríamos.
